meta.title: City Data
meta.chapterTopics: More About Strings, Dictionaries and Sets

overview.body: Your Module 3 code is functional, but it has a problem that every working developer eventually hits: hardcoded data. You have 20+ individual distance constants, city-type checks baked into if/elif chains, and no easy way to add a new city without touching five different places in the file. This week, you fix all of that in one move. CITY_DISTANCES and CITY_INFO are two dictionaries that replace every one of those constants. Adding a new city goes from a multi-file, multi-section change to a single dictionary entry. That's what data-driven design looks like in practice.

mentorQuote.quote: If you had to add 20 more cities, how long would that take with your current code? Now look at a dictionary. That's your answer.

objectives: Create a nested dictionary to store city-to-city distances
Create a second dictionary to store city type, tagline, and win status
Refactor helper functions to use dictionary lookups instead of hardcoded constants
Use the .get() method for safe key access with a fallback value
Understand why dictionaries scale better than parallel constants
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 9: Program 9-1 (card_dealer.py) shows dictionary creation, key access, and looping with random.choice(). The same patterns apply directly to CITY_DISTANCES.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 8 & 9
programRequirements: CITY_DISTANCES is a nested dictionary replacing all individual distance constants
CITY_INFO is a dictionary with city type, tagline, and win status for each city
display_destinations() reads destinations from CITY_DISTANCES, not hardcoded values
get_destination_by_number() returns a city name and distance from the dictionary
is_win_city() and is_dead_end() use CITY_INFO lookups, not hardcoded city names
Top-of-file comment block includes name, abc123, section, and description

namedConstants.name: CITY_DISTANCES
namedConstants.description: Nested dict — city name → {destination: miles}. Replaces all individual distance constants from Module 3.

namedConstants.name: CITY_INFO
namedConstants.description: Dict — city name → {type, tagline}. Stores city classification and display text.

namedConstants.name: STARTING_CITY
namedConstants.description: String constant — always 'San Antonio'. Still a named constant, not hardcoded.

overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-1.py this week. Your function_library.py from Module 3 does not change this week — no need to resubmit it.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep lab-4-1.py — Module 4.2 adds string method input handling directly on top of this dictionary foundation.

rows.task: Build the nested distances dictionary, replacing all constants
Build the city info dictionary with type and tagline
Test every city, clean up, and submit

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your program changes this week. Every [NEW] item is something you are adding. Every [REPLACED] item is something that changes from your 3.3 code. Everything else in your game loop stays the same.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ---- (top of file)
STARTING_CITY   = 'San Antonio'          (unchanged)
CITY_DISTANCES  = { ... }                [NEW -- replaces 20+ distance constants]
CITY_INFO       = { ... }                [NEW -- replaces city-type if/elif chains]

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS ----
display_destinations(current_city)       [REPLACED -- now reads from CITY_DISTANCES]
get_destination_by_number(city, num)     [NEW -- returns name + distance from dict]
is_win_city(city_name)                   [NEW -- checks CITY_INFO['type'] == 'WIN']
is_dead_end(city_name)                   [NEW -- checks CITY_INFO['type'] in dead ends]

# (all your Module 3 functions stay unchanged)
roll_d20(), flip_coin(), etc.            (from function_library -- no changes this week)

GAME LOOP:  (structure unchanged -- only the data source changes)
|
|-- Display city tagline from CITY_INFO   [REPLACED]
|-- Call display_destinations()           [REPLACED -- uses dict now]
|-- Get player choice (get_valid_int)     (unchanged)
|-- Call get_destination_by_number()      [REPLACED -- was hardcoded]
|
|   DESTINATION GATE: (coin flip -- unchanged until 4.3)
|
|   TRAVEL LOOP: (unchanged)
|   |-- Roll D20, accumulate miles
|   |-- 30% hazard check
|   |-- Repeat until arrival
|
|-- Call is_win_city()                    [REPLACED]
|-- Call is_dead_end()                    [REPLACED]
|-- Update stats, check keep_playing

content.body: You are creating a nested dictionary that maps every city to its available destinations and the driving distance in miles. Once this is in place, CITY_DISTANCES['San Antonio']['Austin'] returns 80 — no constant required.

references.description: Shows dictionary creation, key access with bracket notation, and looping through keys. The same patterns apply directly to CITY_DISTANCES.

items: Delete (or comment out) all of your individual distance constants from Module 3 — the lines like SAT_AUS = 80, SAT_HOU = 200, and so on.
Near the top of your file, create CITY_DISTANCES as an empty dictionary: CITY_DISTANCES = {}.
Add San Antonio as the first outer key. Its value is another dictionary with each destination city as a key and the mileage as an integer value.
Add the remaining nine cities as outer keys, each with their own inner destination dictionary. Use the city and mileage data provided in the code plan.
Test a few lookups in isolation before continuing: print(CITY_DISTANCES['San Antonio']['Austin']) should print 80.

content.title: Build It in Pieces
content.body: Don't try to write all ten cities at once. Add San Antonio, test it, then add Houston, test again. A working two-city dictionary is better than a broken ten-city one.

content.title: Keys Are Case-Sensitive
content.body: Dictionary keys must match exactly — capitalization, spaces, and all. 'San Antonio' and 'san antonio' are different keys. Pick one convention and stick to it throughout the entire file.

You are creating a second dictionary that stores each city's type (WIN, HUB, GATEWAY, etc.) and its tagline. This replaces every hardcoded city-type check in your game loop with a clean dictionary lookup.

items: Below your CITY_DISTANCES constant, create CITY_INFO = {}.
For each of the ten cities, add an entry where the key is the city name (spelled exactly as in CITY_DISTANCES) and the value is a dictionary with two keys: 'type' and 'tagline'.

Use these city types and taglines from the code plan: San Antonio (START, 'Home of the Alamo'), Houston (GATEWAY, 'Space City'), Austin (CORRIDOR, 'Keep it weird'), Corpus Christi (WIN, 'The Sparkling City by the Sea'), Galveston (WIN, 'The Playground of the South'), Laredo (DEAD_END, 'Gateway to Mexico'), El Paso (WRONG_WAY, 'Closer to California than Houston'), Dallas (HUB, 'Where everything is bigger'), Fort Worth (HUB, 'Where the West begins'), Texarkana (DEAD_END, 'Two states at once').
Test a lookup: print(CITY_INFO['Austin']['tagline']) should print Keep it weird.

content.title: Why a Nested Dict Instead of Two Separate Ones?
content.body: You could have separate CITY_TYPES and CITY_TAGLINES dictionaries, but grouping all city data under one outer key keeps related information together. When you add a new city, you add one entry — not two.
items: Easier to maintain — one place to update
Easier to extend — add a new field without touching existing code
Clearer intent — CITY_INFO['Austin'] returns everything about Austin

content.body: After this checkpoint, you have two dictionaries defined near the top of your file. Neither is connected to the game loop yet — that happens in CP3. Run the file to confirm it loads without errors, then move on.

Now you wire the dictionaries into the game by rewriting four helper functions. Three of them (display_destinations, is_win_city, is_dead_end) already exist in your code from Module 3. You are replacing their implementations. One (get_destination_by_number) is new.

items: display_destinations(current_city): Use CITY_DISTANCES[current_city] to get the destination dictionary. Loop through it with a counter to display each option as a numbered menu. Return list(CITY_DISTANCES[current_city].keys()) so the game loop knows what options are available.

get_destination_by_number(current_city, choice_num): Get list(CITY_DISTANCES[current_city].keys()), then index into it with choice_num - 1. Return the destination city name and its mileage as two separate values.
is_win_city(city_name): Use CITY_INFO.get(city_name, {}) to safely fetch the city's data, then check if city_data.get('type', '') equals 'WIN'. Return True or False.
is_dead_end(city_name): Same pattern as is_win_city, but check if the type is in ['DEAD_END', 'WRONG_WAY'].
Update your game loop to display the city tagline from CITY_INFO at the start of each turn: tagline = CITY_INFO.get(current_city, {}).get('tagline', '').
Run the game and play through at least one full turn. Confirm the destination menu appears correctly and distances match the dictionary values.

content.title: Use .get() for Safe Lookups
content.body: When looking up a city in CITY_INFO, use .get(city_name, {}) instead of direct bracket access. If a city somehow isn't in the dictionary, you get an empty dict back instead of a KeyError that crashes the program.
items: CITY_INFO['Atlantis'] — raises KeyError, program crashes
CITY_INFO.get('Atlantis', {}) — returns empty dict, program continues

content.quote: I just replaced like fifteen lines of elif chains with four lines of dictionary lookups. I know this is obvious to everyone else but I'm having a moment over here.

rows: Using the old constants alongside the new dict
Confusing source of truth — distance from where?
Delete or comment out all old constants before testing
City name in game state doesn't match dict key exactly
Print current_city and compare to dict keys character by character
Off-by-one error — player enters 1, index is 0
Use choice_num - 1 when indexing into the keys list
Returned list is empty or function isn't being called
Check that current_city is spelled exactly as a key in CITY_DISTANCES

content.body: Before submitting, run through a complete test of your dictionary integration. The game should behave identically to your Module 3 version from a player's perspective — the only thing that changed is where the data lives.

items: Start the game and confirm that San Antonio's tagline ('Home of the Alamo') displays correctly.
Confirm that the destination menu for San Antonio shows all five options with the correct mileage values pulled from CITY_DISTANCES.
Travel to Austin and confirm that Austin's tagline and available destinations appear correctly.
Travel to Corpus Christi and confirm that is_win_city() triggers the win condition.
Travel to Laredo and confirm that is_dead_end() triggers correctly.
Confirm that no hardcoded distance constants appear anywhere in your code. Search for any numeric literals that look like mileage values.

content.title: You're Done When...
content.body: Every city lookup goes through CITY_DISTANCES or CITY_INFO. There are no hardcoded distances anywhere in your game loop or helper functions. The game plays exactly like your Module 3 version — the dictionaries are invisible to the player.

beforeYouSubmit: CITY_DISTANCES is defined with all ten cities and correct mileage values
CITY_INFO is defined with all ten cities, each with 'type' and 'tagline' keys
All old individual distance constants have been removed or commented out
display_destinations() reads from CITY_DISTANCES, not hardcoded values
get_destination_by_number() returns city name and distance from CITY_DISTANCES
is_dead_end() checks CITY_INFO['type'] in dead-end types
City tagline displays at the start of each turn using CITY_INFO
Top-of-file comment block includes name, abc123, section, and description
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: You may use any technique from Chapters 2 through 9 that has been introduced so far. This week's focus is Chapter 9 dictionaries. String methods like .upper() and .strip() are technically in Chapter 8 but are being saved for Module 4.2 — do not use them yet.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (Chapters 2–7 fully permitted from previous modules; Chapter 9 dictionaries as introduced this week; Chapter 8 string methods arrive in Module 4.2)
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: .upper(), .lower(), .strip() (Module 4.2), sets (Module 4.3), list comprehensions, try/except, classes, inheritance

warnings: Do not use .upper() or .strip() yet — those are the Module 4.2 reveal. CodeGrade's scope check will flag premature use of string methods.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep lab-4-1.py — Module 4.2 adds string method input handling directly on top of this dictionary foundation. You will copy this file and build on it.

ifYouGetStuck: Open your textbook to Program 9-1 (card_dealer.py) and trace through how the deck dictionary is built and accessed — it's the same pattern as CITY_DISTANCES
If you're getting a KeyError, print your current_city variable right before the lookup and compare it character by character to your dictionary keys
Build and test one dictionary before starting the other — confirm CITY_DISTANCES works completely before writing CITY_INFO
Submit to CodeGrade early — the test output shows exactly which lookup is failing and what value it expected
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 9 BookEx Reference Guide (bookex-ch09.py) — step-by-step breakdown of Program 9-1. The card deck dictionary is structurally identical to CITY_DISTANCES.
